coredata.dc:title: Simple method to prepare fluorescent silicon rubber by melt-compounding with crude carbon dots fluid
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Guangzhi
Lu, Yonglai
Yu, Peng
Zhang, Liqun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention as fluorescent agents for elastomer composites. Bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from molecular precursors would inevitably produce crude carbon dots (CCDs), i.e., a mixture of CDs, quasi CDs, polymer clusters, and molecular fluorophores. The CCDs are normally subjected to purification processes to obtain the pure CDs, while the purification procedures are time-consuming, expensive, and difficult to be scale-up. Herein, we directly used the CCDs as the fluorescent agents for silicon rubber. To achieve a good CCDs dispersion within the polymer matrix, we firstly prepared the CCDs fluid by mixing the CCDs solid with water, then the CCDs fluid was melt-mixed with the SiR (with the silica as filler) by a two-roll mill. The results revealed that small amounts of CCDs (less than 1 phr) could endow the matrix with intriguing luminescent property. Overall, this work offers a green and facile strategy to use CCDs as low-cost fluorescent agents for the SiR through melt-milling of CCDs fluid and rubber, which can be extended to a variety of other melt-processable polymer matrices (such as millable polyurethane rubber) and enables a broad range of potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent silicon rubber
Crude carbon dots (CCDs)
Melt-compounding
Tensile-sensing
UV-shielding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CCDs fluid and rubber composites
Visual inspection of the rubber composites and CCDs dispersion
RPA analysis and Mechanical properties for the SiR/χCCDs composites
The use of SiR/χCCDs for UV-shielding and tensile-sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation route for the CCDs fluid.

(a) FTIR spectrum of CCDs. (b) UV–vis spectra of CCDs. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CCDs with different excitation wavelengths. (d) 365-nm UV exposure of CCDs solution (0.001 g/mL). (e, f) The TEM images of CCDs. (g) AFM height images and (h) corresponding thickness analysis.
Visual inspection of rubber films: (a) SiR. (b) SiR/0.067CCDs. (c) SiR/0.133CCDs. (d) SiR/0.2CCDs. (e) SiR/0.267CCDs. (f) SiR/0.333CCDs. (g) SiR/0.4CCDs. (h) SiR/0.5CCDs.
The UV–vis curves for SiR/χCCDs films (thickness of 0.6 mm).
Optical microscopy images of the rubber films: (a) SiR. (b) SiR/0.067CCDs. (c) SiR/0.133CCDs. (d) SiR/0.2CCDs. (e) SiR/0.267CCDs. (f) SiR/0.333CCDs. (g) SiR/0.4CCDs. (h) SiR/0.5CCDs.
Schematic diagram of the preparation for SiR/χCCDs composites.

Digital images before UV exposure (A) and under 365 nm-UV exposure (B) for the rubber samples. (a) SiR. (b) SiR/0.067CCDs. (c) SiR/0.133CCDs. (d) SiR/0.2CCDs. (e) SiR/0.267CCDs. (f) SiR/0.333CCDs. (g) SiR/0.4CCDs. (h) SiR/0.5CCDs. (C) The PL curves for the rubber composites. (excited wavelength 360 nm).
The RPA analysis for the uncured SiR/χCCDs composites: (a) G', (b) G”, (c) Tan δ, and (d) Stress-Strain curves for the cured SiR/χCCDs composites.
The UV-blocking effect of the rubber films. (a) SiR film. (b) SiR/0.5CCDs film.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the rubber films at different strain ratio: (a) SiR. (b) SiR/0.267CCDs. (c) SiR/0.5CCDs. (d) The fluorescent intensity changed upon the film stretching.

(a, b) The stress-strain curves and the related fluorescence intensity at various strain ratio for the rubber films. (c, d) Correlation between fluorescence intensity and stress of the rubber films, the red dash corresponds to the linear fittings (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Simple method to prepare fluorescent silicon rubber by melt-compounding with crude carbon dots fluid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Organic–Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Organic–Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Organic–Inorganic Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention as fluorescent agents for elastomer composites. Bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from molecular precursors would inevitably produce crude carbon dots (CCDs), i.e., a mixture of CDs, quasi CDs, polymer clusters, and molecular fluorophores. The CCDs are normally subjected to purification processes to obtain the pure CDs, while the purification procedures are time-consuming, expensive, and difficult to be scale-up. Herein, we directly used the CCDs as the fluorescent agents for silicon rubber. To achieve a good CCDs dispersion within the polymer matrix, we firstly prepared the CCDs fluid by mixing the CCDs solid with water, then the CCDs fluid was melt-mixed with the SiR (with the silica as filler) by a two-roll mill. The results revealed that small amounts of CCDs (less than 1 phr) could endow the matrix with intriguing luminescent property. Overall, this work offers a green and facile strategy to use CCDs as low-cost fluorescent agents for the SiR through melt-milling of CCDs fluid and rubber, which can be extended to a variety of other melt-processable polymer matrices (such as millable polyurethane rubber) and enables a broad range of potential applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), possessing highly desirable and tunable properties (e.g., fascinating photoluminescence and biocompatibility), rendering them promising candidates for wide applications in sensors, disease therapy and bioimaging [1]. CDs also have attracted increasing attention as luminescent additives for various host matrices over the last 10 years [2,3], since embedding or immobilizing CDs in host matrices have the advantage of expanding on various technologies that cannot operated in solution, such as LEDs or photovoltaic devices [4]. The effective use of CDs is highly relying on their incorporation into a solid-state matrix [3], and the “key point” is how to achieve a fine CDs dispersion within the host matrices, since the CDs aggregation would generally induce the luminescence quenching. Many strategies have been reported to fabricate fluorescent polymer/CDs composites, including blending polymers with CDs [5,6], in situ polymerization of polymers in the presence of CDs [7–9], and one-step synthesis of polymer/CDs composites [2]. Nevertheless, the abovementioned strategies might be only adapted for laboratory applications because they are solvent-intensive, time-consuming and difficult to be scale-up. Considering industrial-scale manufacturing of fluorescent polymer/CDs composites is the final target, melt processing is obviously the most promising technique, because the final product can be readily shaped by injection, extrusion or compression-molding [10]. Unfortunately, there is still scarce effort devoted to the development of fluorescent polymer/CDs composites based on melt mixing [11].

Another challenge of the practical application of CDs is the complicated purification process [12]. Synthetic methods of CDs are roughly divided into two categories, “top-down” and “bottom-up” routes [1]. The former employs harsh physical or chemical approaches to break bulk carbon sources into smaller units, whereas the latter involves synthesizing CDs from small molecular precursors, such as polymers and small organic molecules through hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, microwave synthetic, and supported synthetic strategies [13]. Compared with “top-down” synthetic route, the “bottom-up” route is a more promising route for large-scale synthesis of CDs [14]. The CDs formation in “bottom-up” route generally involves complicated ionization/polymerization of the precursors [15] and following carbonization [16], meaning that “bottom-up” route would inevitably produce the crude carbon dots (CCDs) [17,18], i.e., a complex mixture of molecular fluorophores, oligomers, polymer chains, and polymer clusters, quasi CDs, CDs [19]. In most previous studies, the by-products (including molecular fluorophores, polymer clusters, etc.) were commonly removed from CCDs to obtain the pure CDs via post-fractionations by using dialysis, centrifugation or chromatograph techniques [11,20]. However, these procedures are expensive, heavy-workload, time-consuming and difficult to be scale-up. For instance, the dialysis process generally takes several days, and it would produce large quantities of wastewater. Also, it has reported that the by-products generated alongside CDs also possess strong luminescent properties [19,21–24], recent researches even claim that these by-products determine the fluorescent properties of the CCDs [18,25–27]. Up to date, the use of CCDs in polymer composites is far from fully exploited, and we believe that the CCDs, without energy- and time-intensive purification process, could be directly used as a low-cost and effective luminescent agents for polymer composites.

Herein, we attempted to directly use the CCDs as the fluorescent agents to prepare fluorescent elastomer composite based on melt mixing. The commercially available SiR with the silica as filler, which can be processed on standard rubber processing equipment, was chosen as the polymer matrix. We fabricated CCDs from citric acid monohydrate and ethylenediamine by the microwave assisted synthesis, the as-prepared CCDs solid is a rigid glassy-like material, which is difficult to be directly incorporated into the host polymer by the melt mixing. In order to resolve this problem, the CCDs fluid was deliberately prepared by simply mixing the CCDs and water, and it was found that the CCDs fluid could easily compounded with SiR by a two-roll mill, and the small amounts of water in the fluid can be evaporated during the open milling and hot-pressing exhaust processes. Based on this strategy, a fine CCDs dispersion within the matrix could be achieved, which consequently endow the SiR with excellent luminescent property. We envisage that this scalable and green strategy might pave the way of industrial-scale utilization of CCDs in the polymer composites.

SiR containing 0.03 %～0.06 % vinyl groups (Trademark: 110-0) was purchased from Hoshine Silicon Industry Co., Ltd. Silica (Trademark: aerosil 200) was purchased from Evonik Degussa Co., Ltd. 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China) and Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. The rubber additives were purchased from local sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CCDs fluid and rubber composites

ce:section.ce:para: Amine-passivated CCDs were prepared by a facile microwave assisted synthesized method, in detail, 7.7 g citric acid monohydrate and 2.24 g ethylenediamine were dissolved in 70 mL of 0.002 M KH2PO4 aqueous solution. The mixture solution was subjected to the microwave irradiation at 700 W for 9 min, the water was evaporated and the CCDs solid were obtained. After that, the CCDs fluid was prepared by mixing the CCDs solid with a desired amount of water (the solid content of CCDs fluid was adjusted to 60 wt％). The preparation route for CCDs fluid is shown in Fig. 1 , and the Tyndall effect for the CCDs fluid can be observed, indicating the CCDs can be well dispersed/dissolved in the water.

A given amount of silica, silicone oil, CCDs fluid and cure agent (2,5-Dimethyl-2,5-di(tert-butylperoxy)hexane) were incorporated into the SiR matrix by a two-roll mill. Lastly, the well-mixed rubber compounds were hot-pressed at 160 °C for 15 min. The rubber composites were named as SiR/χCCDs, χ refers to the phr of CCDs in the compounds (phr refers to parts per hundred of rubber). The detailed compounding formulation is shown as follows: SiR, 100 phr; silica, 30 phr; CCDs fluid, variation; curing agent, 2,5-Dimethyl-2,5-di-(tert-butylperoxy) hexane, 0.5 phr; silicon oil, 5 phr; silane coupling agent A-151, vinyltrimethoxysilane, 3 phr.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 infrared spectrum analyzer (BRUKER OPTICS, Germany) with KBr pellets. A Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer was used to record UV–vis spectra of the CCDs and rubber films, and the PL spectra were acquired using a FS5 Spectrofluorometer from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-3010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The sample was prepared by dropping diluted CCDs aqueous solution onto a copper grid coated with carbon film. Optical microscope investigations were conducted on a LEICA DM4M (LEICA Co., Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by an atomic force microscope (Nanoscope IIIa, Bruker Corporation, Germany) using the tapping mode in air at ambient temperature, the sample was prepared by depositing the CCDs solution on mica surface and drying at room temperature. Rubber process analysis (RPA) was carried out in a RPA2000 (Alpha technologies Co, USA). During the strain sweeps, the temperature and frequency were kept at 60 °C and 60 cpm, respectively. The range of the strain sweeps was 0～200 %. Tensile tests were conducted on a CMT4104 testing machine (Shenzhen SANS Testing Machine Co. Ltd., China) with a cross-head speed of 500 mm s−1 at room temperature.

Firstly, we investigated the physico-chemical properties of CCDs. The FTIR clearly demonstrate the CCDs are functionalized with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine moieties (Fig. 2 a), these groups account for the excellent water dispersibility of CCDs. Fig. 2b illustrates the UV–vis spectra for CCDs, the CCDs exhibit two absorption peaks around 240 and 340 nm, which are corresponding to the π→π* and n→π* transitions of certain fluorophores in CCDs [28]. In Fig. 2c, the photoluminescence properties of CCDs were investigated by UV excitation with the wavelengths from 320 to 420 nm (with an increasing unit of 20 nm). The optimal excitation of emission of CCDs is 360 nm, corresponding to the optimal emission spectrum of CCDs peak around 450 nm. Fig. 2d shows the emitted bright blue fluorescence of CCDs when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. The TEM (Fig. 2e and f) and AFM (Fig. 2g and h) was used to characterize the morphology of CCDs, we could observe that CCDs particles exhibit quasi-spherical shapes with a wide size distribution, which ranges from few nanometers to decades of nanometer, demonstrating the CCDs are a rather complicated system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual inspection of the rubber composites and CCDs dispersion

ce:section.ce:para: SiR, with silica as the reinforcing filler, is one of mostly widely used transparent elastomers. The incorporation of CCDs is expected to impart intriguing fluorescent properties to SiR. As shown in Fig. 7 , the SiR/χCCDs series exhibited strong fluorescence under UV exposure (Fig. 7B), the fluorescence was increased with the increase of CCDs (Fig. 7C), which is ascribe to the fine CCDs dispersion within the matrix. Moreover, the formative H-bonding between hydroxyl groups of silica and hydroxyl/carboxyl/amide groups of CCDs surface, suppressed the nonradiative decay processes, thus resisting the aggregation-caused-quenching of the CCDs fluorescence [32]. The fluorescence is in accordance with the optical observation of CCDs dispersion discussed above. We also prepared the SiR/CCDs composites without silica, and these composites exhibited no florescence (see Supporting Information), which clearly demonstrated that the silica could facilitate the florescence of the CCDs in the silicon matrix. We also attempted to use the CCDs fluid as the fluorescent agents for the millable polyurethane rubber by the conventional two-roll milling technique, and the ensuing polyurethane/CCDs composites also exhibit strong fluorescence (see Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RPA analysis and Mechanical properties for the SiR/χCCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The rheologic properties and mechanical properties of the rubber composites are shown in Fig. 8 . It is observed that the inclusion of small amounts of CCDs almost do not affect rheologic properties for the SiR/χCCDs composites (Fig. 8a, b, c). That is to say, the CCDs mainly act as ”fluorescent dyes”. Interestingly, we could observe that there are a small maximum of G' at low strains, this is because the filler networks (silica networks) was slightly disturbed, and the silica filler was more exposed to the rubber, which would increase the rubber-filler interaction, thereby increase the G'. When the strain amplitude was progressively increased, G' started to decrease. This can be explained by the Payne effect, i.e., there will be deformation-induced changes in the microstructure and the trapped rubber will be ‘free’, and the G' drop to a very low value. The polymer matrix can not only disperse the CCDs to prevent solid-state quenching but also provide mechanical support [33], the stress-strain curves of rubber composites are shown in Fig. 8d, as expected, the small amounts of CCDs almost have no effect to the mechanical performances of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The use of SiR/χCCDs for UV-shielding and tensile-sensing
ce:section.ce:para: As the concerns of human health care continue increase, particularly focusing on human eyes, skin, immune system and biological systems, UV-blocking materials have garnered increasing attention [34]. It was reported that the CDs can be used as effective UV-blocking agents as they could absorb ultraviolet and emit fluorescence [35]. The SiR film (Fig. 9 a) and SiR/0.5CCDs film (Fig. 9b) were placed between a vial of 0.01 g/mL CCDs aqueous solution and UV light, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9a, it is observed that the CCDs solution emitted bright fluorescence, indicating the UV light pass through the SiR samples and irradiated the CCDs solution. In contrast, Fig. 9b shows the CCDs exhibited very weak fluorescence while the SiR/0.5CCDs film exhibit strong fluorescence, demonstrating that the SiR/0.5CCDs film could effectively shield ultraviolet radiation.

Optical tensile sensing of mechano-responsive elastomers has attracted significant scientifical interest in recent years, these materials could change their fluorescence emission in response to mechanical transformations. Recent work has demonstrated that the rubber/CDs films can be used for fluorescent tensile sensing and mechano-optical tuning [36]. Herein, we attempted to study the stress-fluorescence relationship of SiR/χCCDs films based on the distance-dependent fluorescence characteristics of CCDs. As shown in Fig. 10 , the samples of SiR, SiR/0.267CCDs and SiR/0.5CCDs were subjected to fluorescent testing at various strain ratio, it is obvious, when the rubber films are stretched, the fluorescence intensity decreased for SiR sample (Fig. 10a). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity increased for SiR/0.267CCDs (Fig. 10b) and SiR/0.5CCDs (Fig. 10c) composites. It is known that both the SiR matrix and CCDs could emit fluorescence under UV-irradiation, and the fluorescence for CCDs is much stronger than for SiR. There are at least three factors regulating the fluorescence intensity of the rubber composites upon stretching: (i) The rubber film would become thinner under extension, which will inevitably weaken the florescence. (ii) The CCDs are featured with aggregation-induced quenching, when the rubber/CCDs films are stretched, the distance between adjacent CCDs are increased, thus giving rise to lesser fluorescence quenching, i.e., a more pronounced fluorescence emission. (iii) When the rubber film was stretched, the CCDs were in a more confined surrounding, which could suppress the nonradiative decay processes, resulting in a stronger fluorescence emission [32]. For the SiR/0.267CCDs and SiR/0.5CCDs samples, the last two effects play the dominate role, thus we observed that the fluorescence was enhanced upon stretching.

We further investigated the fluorescence characteristics and mechanical properties for the SiR/0.267CCDs and SiR/0.5CCDs films, interesting, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and stress for the samples under different strains (Fig. 11 c, d). The linearity is manifested in the high R2 factor of 0.961 and 0.953 for SiR/0.267CCDs and SiR/0.5CCDs films, respectively. To a certain degree, this result shows that there is an intrinsic relationship between fluorescence intensity and stress/strain for the film, which allows possible optical determination of the tensile properties.

In this paper, novel elastomer/CCDs composites were developed, for the first time, by introducing CCDs fluid (the CCDs was dissolved in water) into solid rubber via melt processing technology routinely used in industry, the well-dispersed CCDs could endow the elastomer with excellent intriguing fluorescence property without sacrificing mechanical properties. In addition, the as-prepared films showed potential applications in UV-shielding and fluorescent tensile sensing. Overall, this work offers a green and simple strategy to use CCDs fluid as low-cost fluorescent agents for the melt-processable polymers through melt-milling of CCDs fluid and polymer matrix, which will stimulate further research in this thriving field.
Guangzhi Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Performed sample preparation and data analysis.

Writing - original draft. Yonglai Lu: Investigation, Supervision. Peng Yu: Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Liqun Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51790501) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2021.102413.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small molecular organic nanocrystals resemble carbon nanodots in terms of their properties
